prompt: Sort each cost into its Cost of Quality category.

derived.poolL: ['Team training','Design reviews','Quality planning','Supplier evaluation','Product inspection','Quality audits','In-process testing','Tool calibration','Rework before release','Scrapped units','Re-test after fix','Defect downtime','Warranty claims','Product recall','Handling complaints','Field repairs']
derived.poolW: ['Preventing defects up front (training) is a prevention cost.','Reviewing designs to stop defects before they occur is prevention.','Planning quality in before work starts is prevention.','Vetting suppliers to avoid defective inputs is prevention.','Inspecting output to find defects is an appraisal cost.','Auditing to measure conformance is appraisal.','Testing during production to catch defects is appraisal.','Calibrating measuring tools so appraisal is accurate is an appraisal cost.','A defect caught and reworked before the customer sees it is internal failure.','Discarded defective work found internally is internal failure.','Re-testing after an internal fix is internal failure.','Lost time caused by internal defects is internal failure.','A defect that reaches the customer and comes back is external failure.','Recalling shipped product is the costliest failure — external.','Dealing with customer complaints after delivery is external failure.','Repairing product at the customer site is external failure.']

explanation: Cost of Quality splits into conformance costs (Prevention + Appraisal — spent to avoid and catch defects) and non-conformance costs (Internal + External failure). Prevention is cheapest; external failure — defects reaching the customer — is the most expensive.

prompt: Sort each item into Enterprise Environmental Factors (conditions the team does NOT control) or Organizational Process Assets (the organization’s own plans, processes and knowledge).

tokens.label: 'Government regulations'
tokens.why: 'Laws and regulations are external conditions the team must work within — an EEF.'

tokens.label: 'Market conditions'
tokens.why: 'The market is outside the team’s control — an EEF.'

tokens.label: 'Organizational culture'
tokens.why: 'Culture and structure are internal EEFs — conditions, not reusable assets.'

tokens.label: 'Lessons-learned repository'
tokens.why: 'Historical information the team can reuse and update is an OPA.'

tokens.label: 'Templates & checklists'
tokens.why: 'The org’s templates and checklists are process assets — OPA.'

tokens.label: 'Change-control procedures'
tokens.why: 'The organization’s own procedures are OPAs the team applies.'

explanation: EEFs are conditions the team works within but does not control (regulations, market, culture, systems). OPAs are the organization’s own assets the team can use and update (templates, procedures, historical information, lessons learned).

prompt: Sort each response strategy by whether it addresses a threat or an opportunity.

tokens.label: 'Avoid'
tokens.why: 'Avoid eliminates the threat by removing the cause — a threat strategy.'

tokens.label: 'Mitigate'
tokens.why: 'Mitigate reduces a threat’s probability or impact.'

tokens.label: 'Transfer'
tokens.why: 'Transfer shifts a threat’s impact to a third party (insurance, contract).'

tokens.label: 'Exploit'
tokens.why: 'Exploit makes sure an opportunity happens — the mirror of Avoid.'

tokens.label: 'Enhance'
tokens.why: 'Enhance increases an opportunity’s probability or impact — mirror of Mitigate.'

tokens.label: 'Share'
tokens.why: 'Share allocates an opportunity to a partner best able to capture it — mirror of Transfer.'

explanation: Threats get Avoid / Mitigate / Transfer; opportunities get Exploit / Enhance / Share. (Accept and Escalate apply to both.) Note the mirror: Avoid↔Exploit, Mitigate↔Enhance, Transfer↔Share.

prompt: Sort each response strategy by whether it addresses a threat or an opportunity.
caption: Two circles — the overlap is for strategies that apply to both.

tokens.label: 'Avoid'
tokens.why: 'Avoid eliminates the threat by removing its cause — a threat-only strategy.'

tokens.label: 'Mitigate'
tokens.why: 'Mitigate reduces a threat probability or impact — threat only.'

tokens.label: 'Transfer'
tokens.why: 'Transfer shifts a threat impact to a third party — threat only.'

tokens.label: 'Exploit'
tokens.why: 'Exploit makes sure an opportunity happens — opportunity only.'

tokens.label: 'Enhance'
tokens.why: 'Enhance increases an opportunity probability or impact — opportunity only.'

tokens.label: 'Share'
tokens.why: 'Share allocates an opportunity to a partner best able to capture it — opportunity only.'

tokens.label: 'Accept'
tokens.why: 'Accept (take no proactive action, keep a reserve) applies to BOTH threats and opportunities — the overlap.'

tokens.label: 'Escalate'
tokens.why: 'Escalate (raise it to the right authority) applies to BOTH threats and opportunities — the overlap.'

explanation: Threats get Avoid / Mitigate / Transfer; opportunities get Exploit / Enhance / Share (the mirror pairs). Accept and Escalate apply to BOTH, so they sit in the overlap of the two circles.

prompt: Sort each estimate by the technique it uses.

derived.poolL: ['Like last rollout, ~£50k','Similar site, 3 months','Roughly Project X cost','Same as last upgrade','£200 per test × 40','8 hrs per 10 m² tiled','£1,200 per server × 6','3 days per 1,000 units','Sum every task estimate','Cost each work package','Add up activity costs','Cost each part, total']
derived.poolW: ['Reusing a whole similar project cost is analogous (top-down) estimating.','Judging from one comparable past effort is analogous.','A rough figure taken from a past project is analogous.','Basing it on the last similar job is analogous.','A unit rate times a quantity is parametric estimating.','A productivity rate applied to the amount of work is parametric.','A cost per unit times the number of units is parametric.','A duration rate times the quantity is parametric.','Estimating each piece and adding them up is bottom-up.','Detailed per-work-package estimates rolled up is bottom-up.','Summing all the activity costs is bottom-up.','Costing each component and totalling is bottom-up.']

draw.text: mode==0 ? 'Analogous — top-down, from history' : (mode==1 ? 'Analogous' : 'Top-down, from history')
mode==0 ? 'Parametric — a rate × a quantity' : (mode==1 ? 'Parametric' : 'A rate × a quantity')
mode==0 ? 'Bottom-up — sum the detailed parts' : (mode==1 ? 'Bottom-up' : 'Sum the detailed parts')

explanation: Analogous is fast and top-down, using a whole similar project; Parametric multiplies a unit rate by the amount of work; Bottom-up is the most accurate and effortful, summing detailed estimates for each work package.

derived.poolL: ['Pour footings before walls','Write code before test','Cure concrete before load','Approve contract before work','Design UI before API','Usual review order','Prototype before full build','Paint before flooring','Await regulator permit','Vendor delivers hardware','Third-party API goes live','Client provides content']
derived.poolW: ['Physically required order — mandatory (hard) logic.','You cannot test what is not built — mandatory logic.','A physically enforced wait — mandatory logic.','Contractually required before starting — mandatory logic.','A preferred sequence the team chose — discretionary, revisable to compress.','A preferred but optional order — discretionary logic.','A best-practice preference, not required — discretionary.','A preferred order to avoid mess, not required — discretionary.','Depends on a party outside the project — external.','Relies on an external supplier — external.','Depends on an external provider going live — external.','Waits on the external client — external.']

explanation: Mandatory (hard logic) is inherent in the work and cannot change. Discretionary (preferred/soft logic) is a choice the team can revisit to compress the schedule. External depends on non-project parties (regulators, vendors, clients).

prompt: Drag each situation onto the communication method it needs.

derived.poolL: ['Policy update to 200 staff','Monthly status memo','Broadcast release notes','Compliance notice org-wide','Self-serve metrics dashboard','Docs in a shared wiki','Reports on a portal','Contracts archived for audit','Negotiate with the client','Resolve a team conflict','Brainstorm the design','Kickoff workshop']
derived.poolW: ['One-way to a large audience with no Q&A — push.','A status memo pushed out to everyone — push.','A broadcast that needs no confirmation — push.','A notice distributed org-wide — push.','A large audience retrieves it at will — pull.','Reference material people look up when needed — pull.','Self-serve reports on a portal — pull.','An archive accessed on demand for audit — pull.','A tense two-way negotiation — interactive.','Real-time conflict resolution — interactive.','Live back-and-forth idea generation — interactive.','A real-time workshop to build agreement — interactive.']

explanation: Push = sender distributes with no confirmation (memos, broadcasts). Pull = recipients access at will (portals, wikis, dashboards). Interactive = real-time two-way, essential for negotiation, conflict and building agreement.
